Just now

--

Where Will the Next Generation of Skilled Professionals Come From?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Rookie (Red Sox Locker Room), Norman Rockwell (1957). [1]Since the start of 2023, a strange pattern has appeared in the data on companies that adopt AI. Two Harvard economists tracked tens of millions of workers across hundreds of thousands of firms and found that the ones rolling out generative AI had pulled back sharply on hiring at the bottom, while their senior ranks held steady. One explanation was that these firms were moving junior workers up into more advanced work as the machine took over the routine parts. The researchers looked for that effect and found little evidence of it. The entry-level roles were not being transformed. They were simply not being filled. [2] [3]
Which leaves a quiet problem no dashboard shows. These firms still need senior people, and senior people are made out of junior ones. It had become hard to say where the next generation of experts was supposed to come from.
This is not really a story about any one profession. Every field with a ladder ran on the same arrangement: the boring, codified tasks flowed downhill to whoever had arrived most recently. Junior engineers fixed the small bugs. Junior lawyers read the contracts. Junior analysts built the spreadsheets. And generative AI is very good at exactly this kind of work, work that is codified, checkable, the kind you can learn from a textbook. So the decision to hand it to the machine instead of the beginner looks, on every visible measure, like a good one. The work is tedious, the machine is faster, and no one enjoyed doing it anyway.
The trouble is what those firms backed into. In getting rid of the drudgery, we are also switching off the process by which people used to stop being beginners.
The reflex is to call this a training problem: teach the judgment directly, in a course, and skip the years of grunt work. It sounds sensible until you sit down to write the curriculum. Ask a senior lawyer what makes her uneasy about a contract and she often can’t tell you; she can only tell you she’s read ten thousand of them. We know more than we can tell, [5] and the part we can’t tell is exactly the part that repetition, not instruction, lays down in us. You can’t lecture someone into a reflex. Whatever the junior work was teaching, it wasn’t teaching it in a form you could lift out and hand over. It taught through the doing.
Which points at something we had never needed to notice, because until now it had never come apart. A task can produce two things at once. One is its visible output, such as the reviewed contract, the fixed bug, the finished spreadsheet. The other is a change in the person doing it. The young lawyer reading a thousand contracts is generating a stack of reviewed contracts and, at the same time, slowly becoming someone who can glance at a page and feel that something is wrong before she can say why. The firm thought it was paying for the first. It was also, with nothing on the invoice to say so, installing a profession in a person.
Call the first output productive and the second formative. The difference is precise. The productive output is worth the same even if the person doing the work learns nothing from it. The formative output is the part of the value that vanishes when the person comes out unchanged. Almost every real task carries both at once, which is exactly why we never had to tell them apart. They arrived fused, in the same activity, and we measured the pair by the only one that ever showed up on a ledger.
What is new is not that the two can be separated. Calculators, autopilots, and factory robots each pulled them apart in some narrow place long ago. What is new is the reach. AI brings the separation into the core of knowledge work: the reading, writing, coding, and analysis through which judgment in many professions was quietly grown. For the first time, across a broad range of cognitive work, we can keep the product and drop the formation. And not feel the loss, because only the product was ever counted.
Once you can see the second output, some confident decisions start to look different.
Notice what we did when we set the machine against the beginner. We asked which of them does the task better, and the machine plainly won. But the beginner was never the finished product of the process. The beginner was the process. A beginner is not an inefficient expert; a beginner is how expertise reproduces itself. Measuring one against a machine is like judging an investment as though it were already a return.
And because the return comes late, the mistake is easy to make and hard to catch in time. A firm can improve every number on its dashboard while quietly running down its future supply of judgment. The gain lands this quarter; the loss surfaces years later, as an inability to find anyone ready to be promoted. The failure is delayed, which makes the decision that caused it look, for a long time, like a success.
There is a more general law underneath this, and it reaches well past hiring: some efficiencies consume the conditions that made them possible. AI is trusted in these professions partly because experts are still there to catch it when it errs, and those experts were made by the very process now being automated away. An efficiency that eats its own precondition isn’t really a saving. It’s a loan against a future no one has arranged to repay.
Once you have the idea, you find it far outside AI, wherever a task was doing two jobs at once.
Sometimes the second job is to form a capacity. A student handed the answer has not learned what a student who fought her way to it has, even when they write down the same result. The struggle wasn’t the cost of the answer; it was the making of a mind that can find the next one.
Sometimes it is to maintain one. The reason a pilot keeps flying by hand even with the autopilot on, so the skill is still there on the rare day the automation isn’t. [4]
And sometimes it is to reveal something no one knew in advance. A mediocre first draft, written by you, does what reading a polished one written for you cannot: it is where you find out what you actually think.
Form, maintain, reveal. In each case the visible output is real, it is simply not the only thing being produced. The work was doing something to the person, and only the person would carry it forward.
Which suggests our idea of productivity was always drawn too narrowly. We treat every task as production, as a thing that exists to yield an output even when part of its point was succession: the passing of a capability from one set of people to the next. A profession is not only a way of producing work. It is a way of producing the people who will produce the work after you.
None of this is the machine’s intention. AI has no preference about which output it keeps; it removes whatever we point it at. If formation is the part that gets dropped, the reason is duller and more human than anything about machines: only the product was ever in the metrics. That is the quiet risk: not that we run out of beginners, but that we build companies, schools, and whole professions that produce everything except the people they depend on. They could look efficient the whole way, because the missing thing was never on the books. How close any of them actually are to that, and how much formation each of them truly needs, no one can yet say. We were never counting it.
None of which means every old apprenticeship should be saved; some of what we called formative was drudgery protected by tradition. Nor does it mean AI can only subtract, because it might build judgment in ways the slow years never could. Maybe the bottom rung has to stay. Maybe it can be rebuilt somewhere else, or built better. But we can’t choose until we can see that the work was doing two jobs, and that we only ever learned to measure one.
Every task makes something. Some of them also make someone. We know how to replace the first. We are only beginning to ask what it would take to replace the second.

Notes
[1] The Rookie (Red Sox Locker Room), 1957 by Norman Rockwell. Image © SEPS, licensed by Curtis Licensing. Used under Fair Use guidelines for cultural commentary and educational analysis in relation to the development of entry-level expertise.
[2] The hiring pattern is from Seyed Mahdi Hosseini Maasoum and Guy Lichtinger, “Generative AI as Seniority-Biased Technological Change” (SSRN, August 2025), covering roughly 62 million U.S. workers at more than 280,000 firms, 2015–2025. Using a difference-in-differences design, they find a substantial relative decline in junior employment at firms after they adopt generative AI, driven mainly by slower hiring rather than layoffs or promotions, while senior employment remains largely unchanged.
[3] A separate analysis (Brynjolfsson and colleagues, 2025) finds early-career workers aged 22–25 in the most AI-exposed occupations experienced roughly a 13% relative decline in employment, with employment for software developers in that age group down nearly 20% from its recent peak.
[4] Lisanne Bainbridge, “Ironies of Automation,” Automatica 19(6), 1983. Bainbridge observed that the skills which decay under reliable automation are often the same skills the operator needs when the automation fails.
[5] Michael Polanyi, The Tacit Dimension (1966). “We know more than we can tell” is Polanyi’s formulation of tacit knowledge: knowledge expressed through judgment and practice that cannot be fully articulated as explicit rules.
